Center of the frame: a large cylindrical storage tank (4 meters diameter × 6 meters tall, oriented vertically, resting on a turntable base that allows rotation during application) is being spray-coated. The applicator stands on a hydraulic scissor lift platform at the 3-meter level, operating a Graco or DeVilbiss plural-component spray gun. The two-component vinyl ester resin mixes at the gun nozzle and deposits as a smooth, glossy film. The spray cone is visible — a fine, controlled mist. The applicator wears full PPE: white Tyvek coverall suit, rubber overshoes, latex inner gloves under chemical-resistant outer gloves, and a full-face positive-pressure air-fed respirator (the corrugated breathing air hose trails to a supply point on the lift platform). 

The tank's surface tells the story of the coating process: the lower 60% is completed — a uniform, deep blue-green glossy coating (high-build vinyl ester, approximately 3mm total DFT) with a mirror-like surface finish. The upper 40% shows the intermediate state — the first coat of amber-colored epoxy primer over the near-white-metal blast-cleaned substrate. The boundary line between completed and in-progress is crisp and horizontal — the applicator works downward in controlled bands.

To the right, a stainless steel QC station: a table with calibrated instruments — an Elcometer 456 DFT gauge (the probe on a test block, display showing "2,850 μm"), a surface profile comparator, a holiday detector with its probe coiled, and an environmental monitoring unit (digital display showing temperature 21°C, RH 45%, dew point spread +5°C — all within specification). A laminated coating procedure specification (CPS) is clipped to a board above.

In the background, two additional bays are visible through wide openings: one shows a vessel being blast-cleaned (the blast operator in protective suit visible through a viewing window in the blast chamber wall), the other shows a completed coated vessel with "QC PASSED" documentation attached, ready for dispatch.

German regulatory compliance is visible: safety signage in German (yellow triangular warning signs for "Atemschutz erforderlich" — respiratory protection required), emergency shower stations (green and white signs), fire extinguisher points, and an evacuation plan posted on the wall. A digital safety board near the entrance shows "Unfallfreie Tage: 847" (accident-free days: 847).

The lighting is controlled: high-bay LED fixtures provide even, daylight-balanced illumination (5000K) — essential for inspecting coating quality and color consistency. No shadows, no hot spots, no dark corners. The entire space is lit as evenly as a photography studio, which is actually a coating application requirement.

Shot on a Sony A7R V with a 24-70mm f/2.8 GM II at 28mm, f/5.6. ISO 400, 1/125s. The wide angle captures the full workspace while keeping the applicator and spray operation as the clear focal point. Color: neutral, clinical, precise — the coating colors (blue-green, amber, grey steel) are rendered truthfully without artistic color grading. Documentary industrial photography at its most precise. No AI artifacts. No stock photography.

entries.caption: Global Operations Map
entries.prompt:
A clean, minimal dark-background infographic-style map visualization showing Jasmino Group's three global facility locations. This is a DESIGNED GRAPHIC, not a photograph — it should feel like a premium data visualization from a Fortune 500 annual report.

The background is deep navy (#0B1D34) with a very subtle noise texture (1-2% opacity) to prevent banding. Continental landmasses are rendered as very low-opacity outlines — not filled shapes, just faint border lines (rgba(255,255,255,0.04)) showing the coastlines of Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and South/Southeast Asia. The landmasses are drawn in a clean equirectangular projection, slightly simplified. Ocean areas are pure navy background. A very faint latitude/longitude grid (rgba(255,255,255,0.02), 15-degree spacing) adds subtle structure.

Three facility markers are the focal points — each is a glowing green dot (#04E586) with three concentric rings:
- Inner dot: solid green, 8px diameter
- Middle ring: green at 30% opacity, 20px diameter, 1px stroke
- Outer ring: green at 10% opacity, 36px diameter, 1px stroke
- A subtle radial gradient glow emanates from each dot (green to transparent, 50px radius)

The three locations with labels:

1. TALOJA, MAHARASHTRA, INDIA (positioned correctly on the west coast of India, south of Mumbai):
   Label: "JASMINO INDIA" in monospace, 11px, white at 80% opacity
   Sub-label: "Headquarters · Engineering · Manufacturing · Rubber Products" in monospace, 9px, white at 35% opacity
   Stat: "80,000 m²" in monospace, 13px, green (#04E586)
   A small Indian flag icon (16×11px) sits beside the facility name.

2. BOCKENEM, GERMANY (positioned correctly in Lower Saxony, south of Hannover):
   Label: "HAW LININGS" in monospace, 11px, white at 80% opacity
   Sub-label: "World's Largest Rubber Lining Facility · R&D · Autoclave Curing" in monospace, 9px, white at 35% opacity
   Stat: "120,000 m²" in monospace, 13px, green (#04E586)
   A small German flag icon (16×11px) sits beside the facility name.

3. MARL, GERMANY (positioned correctly in North Rhine-Westphalia, Ruhr region):
   Label: "GBT BÜCOLIT" in monospace, 11px, white at 80% opacity
   Sub-label: "Coatings · Plastic Linings · Field Service" in monospace, 9px, white at 35% opacity
   Stat: "20,000 m²" in monospace, 13px, green (#04E586)
   A small German flag icon (16×11px) sits beside the facility name.

Connecting arcs: three great-circle arc lines connect the facilities:
- India ↔ Bockenem: a smooth quadratic bezier arc (apex approximately 2000km north of the midpoint), stroke: green at 15% opacity, 1.5px width, dashed (8px dash, 4px gap)
- India ↔ Marl: same style arc, slightly different curvature
- Bockenem ↔ Marl: a shorter, lower arc between the two German locations, same style

Bottom bar: a horizontal stat strip centered at the bottom of the image, 60px from the bottom edge:
"130,000+ m² COMBINED" · "15+ COUNTRIES SERVED" · "4 CONTINENTS" · "50+ YEARS COMBINED HERITAGE"
Each stat separated by a thin vertical line (white at 10% opacity). Text: monospace, 10px, white at 40% opacity. Numbers in white at 70% opacity.

The overall feel: dark, precise, authoritative — a map that says "we are HERE, operating at THIS scale, across THESE locations." Premium data visualization quality. Clean anti-aliased rendering. No decorative elements. No gradients on the landmasses. No 3D effects. No stock-map aesthetics.

entries.caption: Manifesto Section Photo
entries.prompt:
Portrait-orientation photograph of the interior of a heavy fabrication workshop — a composition that captures the human scale of industrial craftsmanship.

The frame is filled with the curved wall of a massive pressure vessel under construction — approximately 6 meters in diameter, the carbon steel surface showing the characteristic blue-grey of freshly welded and ground metal. Three circumferential weld seams are visible, each ground flush for radiographic examination — the grinding marks create subtle parallel arcs that catch the overhead light.

In the lower third of the frame, two workers stand on a scaffold platform at the vessel's equator. They are shown from behind — both wearing navy coveralls, white hard hats, and leather welding gauntlet gloves. One holds a welding inspection gauge against a weld seam, checking the cap reinforcement height. The other marks the inspection grid with a yellow wax crayon, writing zone numbers on the steel surface. Their posture is focused, methodical — craftsmen performing exacting work.

The vessel curves away above them, rising out of frame. The scale is unmistakable — the two workers barely reach the vessel's horizontal centerline. Above them, the fabrication hall's steel structure is visible: crane rails, trusses, and translucent skylight panels providing diffused natural light from above.

The lighting is layered: cool diffused daylight from the skylights illuminates the upper vessel surface. Warm tungsten task lights on the scaffold platform create an amber pool around the workers. The contrast between the cool steel and warm work zone creates depth and atmosphere.

Shot on a Hasselblad X2D with an XCD 65mm f/2.8 at f/4. ISO 400. Portrait orientation, medium telephoto compression. The depth of field keeps the workers and vessel surface sharp, with the overhead structure softening into atmospheric depth. Color: desaturated steel-blue with warm amber at the work zone. Documentary industrial photography. Imperfect, real, not CGI. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: Photo Break — Fabrication Panoramic
entries.prompt:
Ultra-wide panoramic photograph of the Jasmino India fabrication floor at Taloja, Maharashtra — a single sweeping image that captures the full breadth of integrated manufacturing capability.

The camera is positioned at the mezzanine level (approximately 6 meters above ground), looking across the full width of the main fabrication bay. The panoramic aspect ratio (approximately 2.3:1) captures the extraordinary horizontal extent of the facility.

Left third: A massive horizontal pressure vessel (7m diameter × 25m long) on welding rotators. An automated SAW welding head deposits a circumferential seam — the subtle orange glow of the flux-covered arc visible. Workers on a two-level scaffold platform perform fit-up checks. The vessel's freshly-primed surface (grey inorganic zinc silicate) catches the overhead light.

Center third: The main assembly area where a vertical vessel (5m diameter × 12m tall) stands on a fabrication skid, surrounded by spiral scaffold. Workers at four levels perform simultaneous operations — nozzle welding at the lower shell, seam grinding at mid-height, lifting lug installation at the top. Two overhead cranes (the 100T yellow and 200T orange, capacity markings visible) are positioned over this vessel.

Right third: The surface preparation and coating zone — a vessel emerging from the shot blast chamber (the heavy steel doors partially open), its surface a uniform silvery white metal. Beyond, the material staging area with cut steel plates on dunnage, a CNC plasma cutting gantry, and the pipe spool fabrication section.

The floor throughout: clean organized concrete with painted yellow walkways, blue forklift lanes, and safety markings. The ceiling — 35 meters above — shows the full truss structure with translucent skylight panels flooding the space with diffused natural light.

The image reads as a single continuous workspace where design, fabrication, surface preparation, and quality inspection happen simultaneously — the INTEGRATED MODEL made physical. Workers appear as small figures throughout, emphasizing the cathedral scale.

Shot on a Hasselblad X2D with an XCD 21mm f/4, panoramic stitch from 3 frames. f/8, ISO 400. Cool steel-blue base with warm tungsten task lights creating amber pools at work stations. Atmospheric haze from welding ventilation adds depth. Documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: Timeline: Today — Global Enterprise
entries.prompt:
A composite aerial photograph showing the global scale of the Jasmino Group today — three facilities, two countries, one unified standard.

The image is composed as a cinematic split: three aerial views blended together with subtle transitions, each showing one facility from approximately 80 meters altitude.

Left section (40% of frame): The Jasmino India campus at Taloja, Maharashtra — the sprawling 80,000 m² complex with its cathedral-scale fabrication halls, engineering office block, rubber products manufacturing area, and dispatch yard with completed vessels on transporters. Late afternoon golden light, Maharashtra landscape, tropical vegetation at the perimeter.

Center section (30% of frame): The HAW Linings facility at Bockenem, Germany — the enormous 120,000 m² campus with its connected production halls, autoclave bay, and organized dispatch yard. Overcast Northern European light, mature deciduous trees, German industrial park setting.

Right section (30% of frame): The GBT Bücolit facility at Marl, Germany — the 20,000 m² coating and lining specialist in the Ruhr chemical corridor. Clean Northern European industrial architecture, adjacent to the Marl chemical park.

The three views are blended with subtle gradient transitions — not hard cuts, but soft overlaps that suggest unity. The consistent aerial perspective (same altitude, same angle) across all three creates visual coherence despite the geographic distance.

The mood is expansive, confident — three facilities on two continents, operating as one group. The golden Indian light transitions through neutral to the cool German overcast, but the industrial character remains consistent.

Shot as three separate drone photographs composited in post. Each section: medium format quality, f/8, natural ambient light. Color: warm gold (India) transitioning to cool grey (Germany). Documentary aerial photography. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: Values: Own the Outcome
entries.prompt:
A photograph that captures the moment of ACCOUNTABILITY — a single point of contact between Jasmino and the client, embodied in the handover of a completed, quality-verified pressure vessel.

The scene: the dispatch yard at the Jasmino India facility. A completed pressure vessel (5m diameter × 15m long, horizontal, in full primer coat with the ASME U-stamp nameplate prominently visible on the shell) sits on a multi-axle hydraulic transporter, chained and blocked for road transport. The vessel is ready to leave the facility — the final step in the integrated process.

In the foreground, two figures stand at the vessel's nameplate: a Jasmino QC manager and a client's inspection representative performing the final document handover. They are shown from behind at a three-quarter angle — the QC manager holds open the Manufacturer's Data Report (MDR) binder (a thick ring binder with tabbed sections visible — material certificates, weld maps, NDE reports, hydrostatic test certificates, dimensional inspection records). The client representative reviews a page, pen in hand ready to sign the final acceptance.

The ASME nameplate on the vessel is legible: stamped with the U symbol, the manufacturer's name, the design pressure and temperature, and the National Board number. Quality inspection tags (green "RELEASED" stickers with QC numbers and dates) are attached at multiple points on the vessel.

Behind them, the fabrication hall looms — bay doors open, another vessel under construction visible inside. The full chain of custody is implied: designed, fabricated, inspected, tested, documented, and now being accepted — all under one roof, one contract, one point of accountability.

Late afternoon light — the vessel's primer surface catches warm sidelight, creating long shadows across the dispatch yard. The mood is quietly proud — the culmination of months of integrated work.

Shot on a Canon EOS R5 with a 50mm f/1.2L at f/4. ISO 200. The nameplate and document handover are sharp, the background hall softens. Color: warm golden sidelight on grey primer, documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: Jasmino: Fabrication Hall
entries.prompt: Interior of the Jasmino India main fabrication hall — a wide shot showing a massive vessel on welding rotators with overhead crane positioned above. Workers on scaffold platforms. Cathedral-scale space with 35m ceiling, steel trusses, skylight panels. The emphasis is SCALE — the vessel dominates, humans are small. Cool steel-blue tones with warm tungsten task lighting. Shot on medium format, f/8, ISO 400. Documentary industrial photography. Desaturated, high contrast. People shown only from behind or as distant figures. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: Jasmino: Engineering Office
entries.prompt: The Jasmino engineering office — dual-monitor workstations showing SP3D plant models and PV Elite vessel designs. Engineers shown from behind. Through the interior window, the fabrication hall is visible with a vessel under construction. Cool screen glow, professional office lighting. The connection between design and production is the story. Shot on full-frame, 35mm f/2.8, ISO 320. Documentary office photography. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: Jasmino: Rubber Mixing
entries.prompt: The rubber compound mixing department — a large Banbury internal mixer in operation, the ram in the down position. An operator at the control panel monitors temperature and mixing energy. A two-roll mill downstream receives the freshly mixed compound — a hot, dark sheet of rubber passing through the nip, steaming slightly. Raw materials staged on pallets: carbon black bags, rubber bales, process oil drums. Warm, slightly hazy industrial atmosphere. Shot on full-frame, 35mm f/4, ISO 800. Documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: Jasmino: Aerial Facility
entries.prompt: Aerial view of the Jasmino India campus at Taloja, Maharashtra from 80 meters altitude. The 80,000 m² complex: fabrication halls with sawtooth roofing, engineering office block, rubber products area, dispatch yard with vessels on transporters. Surrounding Taloja MIDC industrial zone visible. Late afternoon light, long shadows. Shot from drone platform, medium format quality, f/8, ISO 100. Natural color, documentary aerial. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: HAW: Lining Workshop
entries.prompt: Interior of the HAW Linings production hall in Bockenem, Germany — multiple vessels in various stages of the rubber lining process. Foreground: a vessel with manway open, technician inside applying rubber lining. Background: the autoclave bay with large autoclaves visible. Clean, well-lit Northern European industrial space (5000K lighting). The scale of the operation — multiple vessels simultaneously — communicates industrial capability. Shot on medium format, 28mm f/5.6, ISO 400. Cool, clean documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: HAW: Autoclave Curing
entries.prompt: A large autoclave at HAW Linings, Bockenem — the massive 6m diameter end door swung open, revealing the interior. A rubber-lined vessel on a rail trolley is being loaded into the autoclave for vulcanisation curing. Steam supply piping with pressure gauges visible. The control station shows temperature and pressure set-points on digital displays. The scale of the autoclave — large enough to contain a complete process vessel — communicates INDUSTRIAL CURING CAPABILITY. Shot on full-frame, 24mm f/5.6, ISO 400. Cool steel-blue, documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: HAW: Quality Inspection
entries.prompt: A QC inspector at HAW performing holiday detection on a completed rubber lining inside a vessel. The inspector holds a high-voltage holiday detector probe, sweeping it across the glossy black vulcanised rubber surface. The test voltage displayed on the instrument unit. The systematic grid pattern of chalk marks on the lining surface. Inside the vessel — curved walls, portable work lights creating dramatic shadows. Shot on full-frame, 35mm f/2.8, ISO 800. Documentary, dramatic work-light illumination. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: HAW: Bockenem Facility
entries.prompt: Exterior establishing shot of the HAW Linings facility in Bockenem, Lower Saxony, Germany. A substantial Northern European industrial campus — multiple connected production halls totaling 120,000 m². Light grey metal cladding, flat roofs, well-maintained grounds with mature birch trees. The facility sign "HAW LININGS — A Jasmino Group Company" visible. European vehicles in the parking area. Overcast Northern German sky — flat, grey, even illumination. Shot on medium format, 45mm f/8, ISO 200. Cool, documentary exterior. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: GBT: Coating Application
entries.prompt: Interior of the GBT Bücolit coating spray bay in Marl, Germany. An applicator in full PPE (air-fed respirator, Tyvek suit) operates a plural-component spray gun, depositing dark blue-green vinyl ester coating on a large tank interior. The completed coating area is glossy and uniform. Extraction hoods overhead, environmental controls visible. German safety signage. Clean, well-organized European facility. Shot on full-frame, 24mm f/4, ISO 400. Neutral, clinical documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: GBT: Resin Systems Lab
entries.prompt: The quality control and testing area at GBT — a bench with calibrated coating inspection instruments: DFT gauge with probe on a test surface, holiday detector, surface profile comparator, environmental monitoring unit showing temperature and humidity. Coating procedure specifications clipped to a board. Test panels showing different coating systems (epoxy primer amber, vinyl ester blue-green, polyurethane topcoat). Bright laboratory lighting. Shot on full-frame, 50mm f/4, ISO 200. Clinical, precise, documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: GBT: On-Site Crew
entries.prompt: A GBT field service team at work on-site at a chemical plant during a turnaround. Two technicians in GBT-branded coveralls and full PPE work inside an installed vessel — visible through the open manway. Confined space entry equipment (gas testing tripod, harness, ventilation hose) staged at the vessel entrance. Plant infrastructure visible: piping, structural steel, scaffolding. The reality of field lining work. Shot on full-frame, 35mm f/2.8, ISO 800. Mixed plant lighting, documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: GBT: Marl Facility
entries.prompt: Exterior establishing shot of the GBT Bücolit facility in Marl, North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany. An industrial facility in the Ruhr chemical corridor — clean steel-and-cladding buildings, organized yard with vessels on transport cradles. The Marl chemical park is suggested in the background. Company signage "GBT Bücolit — A Jasmino Group Company." Overcast Northern European light. Shot on medium format, 45mm f/8, ISO 200. Cool, clean documentary. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: 1984: Jasmino Founded
entries.prompt: Vintage-style photograph recreating the founding of Jasmino in 1984 at Taloja, Maharashtra, India. A modest single-bay industrial workshop — corrugated steel roof, concrete floor, a small lathe, a manual welding set, steel plates leaning against walls, and rolls of raw rubber compound on wooden pallets. A single engineer at a wooden drafting table, shown from behind, reviewing a hand-drawn vessel drawing. A hand-painted sign reads "JASMINO ELASTOMERS PVT LTD." Natural light floods through the open bay door. Warm, slightly faded color palette suggesting the 1980s era — Kodachrome warmth, visible film grain. The mood is humble beginnings — small scale, big ambition. A ceiling fan turns slowly overhead. Documentary period photography. Not CGI. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: 1968: HAW Established
entries.prompt: Period-style photograph of the early HAW Linings operation in Bockenem, Germany, circa late 1960s. A clean, well-organized German industrial workshop — concrete block walls painted light grey, fluorescent strip lighting overhead. A technician in work clothes applies rubber sheet lining by hand inside a medium-sized chemical vessel (2m diameter), using a stitcher roller. The rubber sheets, adhesive pots, and hand tools are visible on a workbench. The workshop is modest but meticulously orderly — German precision evident even in these early days. Through the workshop window, the flat Lower Saxony landscape is visible. The color palette is muted, cool — Ektachrome blue-green shadows, period film grain. Documentary European industrial photography of the 1960s. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: 2000: Polymertech Era
entries.prompt: Aerial photograph of the Jasmino facility at Taloja circa 2000 — the campus mid-expansion. The original operational buildings on the left (showing 25+ years of use), with new steel-framed fabrication halls under construction on the right — structural steel erected, wall cladding partially installed, construction cranes visible. A paved internal road separates existing and new areas. Workers and industrial vehicles move between buildings. The surrounding Taloja MIDC industrial zone visible at the perimeter. Clear Maharashtra sky, late morning light. The image tells the GROWTH story — a rubber products company transforming into an integrated engineering and manufacturing operation. Shot as documentary aerial, medium format quality. Natural color, no HDR. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: 2018: Partnership Begins
entries.prompt: A business meeting at the HAW Linings conference room in Bockenem, Germany — the moment of strategic partnership formation between Jasmino and European partners. Six to eight executives around a conference table covered with technical drawings of rubber-lined vessels, project photographs from multiple countries, and partnership documents. Through the floor-to-ceiling windows, the HAW facility is visible — clean Northern European industrial buildings. The group is diverse — Indian and German executives in business attire, shown from behind and in profile (no direct faces). A projection screen shows a world map with project locations marked by green dots. The mood is strategic and professional. Warm conference room lighting, professional corporate photography. No AI artifacts.

entries.caption: 2024: Acquisition Completed
entries.prompt: A formal moment at the entrance of the HAW Linings facility in Bockenem, Germany marking the 2024 acquisition. A group of 10-12 senior leaders — Indian and German — stand in front of the facility entrance. The signage now displays both "HAW LININGS" and "JASMINO GROUP" branding. The group is shown from a 30-degree angle — backs, sides, profiles. Some shake hands, one holds a framed document. Behind them, the enormous 120,000 m² facility stretches away — bay doors open showing ongoing operations. Well-maintained grounds, European vehicles, three flags (German, EU, Jasmino Group). Overcast Northern German sky. The mood is proud but professional — an engineering company completing a strategic milestone. Documentary corporate/industrial photography. No AI artifacts.
